data.title: CZ proposed 4 suggestions for optimizing the X comment algorithm, including prohibiting robot automatic replies, etc.
Kentucky's encryption bill completes third reading
data.description: Odaily Planet Daily News Kentucky's crypto bill has completed the third reading, and is one step closer to the governor's signature. Earlier news, Kentucky Congressman TJ Roberts initiated the HB376 bill, proposing to invest 10% of state funds in digital assets (Bitcoin) with a market value of more than $750 billion. Kentucky is also the 16th state to propose a Bitcoin reserve bill. (Cointelegraph) Note: The third reading is a legislative procedure of the legislature. Because the title of the draft bill or motion will be read three times during this procedure, the procedure is called the third reading.

data.title: Kentucky's Cryptocurrency Bill Advances Closer to Governor's Approval
data.description: According to Odaily, a cryptocurrency bill in Kentucky has completed its third reading, moving it closer to being signed by the governor. The legislative process known as the third reading involves the title of the bill being read three times. Previously, Kentucky State Representative TJ Roberts introduced Bill HB376, which proposes investing 10% of the state's funds in digital assets with a market capitalization exceeding $750 billion, such as Bitcoin. Kentucky is the 16th state to propose a Bitcoin reserve bill.

data.title: Bolivia's YPFB Plans Cryptocurrency Use for Energy Imports Amid Currency Shortage
data.description:
According to Cointelegraph, Bolivia's state-owned energy company, YPFB, is preparing to utilize cryptocurrency for energy import payments. This strategic move comes as Bolivia grapples with a significant shortage of foreign currency reserves and a decline in domestic gas production. A YPFB spokesperson confirmed that a system has been established to facilitate cryptocurrency transactions for energy imports, following government approval for the use of digital assets to address the country's energy demands. Although the system has not yet been implemented, YPFB intends to proceed with its use.

The specific cryptocurrency to be used for these transactions has not been disclosed. While stablecoins, which are digital assets tied to fiat currencies, are commonly employed for cross-border transactions, it remains uncertain if Bolivia will adopt this approach. The fuel shortage in Bolivia has sparked protests and potential strikes among workers, including farmers, who express concerns that the lack of fuel could jeopardize their summer harvest. Currently, only 35% to 50% of the nation's public transport system is operational. Alejandro Gallardo, the energy and hydrocarbons minister, highlighted the challenges posed by the foreign currency shortage.

The YPFB spokesperson emphasized that the new purchasing system is designed to support national fuel subsidies amid the foreign currency shortage. "From now on, these cryptocurrency transactions will be carried out," they stated.

In June 2024, Bolivia's central bank, Banco Central de Bolivia, lifted its longstanding ban on Bitcoin and cryptocurrency payments, allowing financial institutions to engage in digital asset transactions. This ban had been in effect since 2014. By September 2024, Bolivia reported a 100% increase in virtual asset trading, with approximately $15.6 million worth of assets traded monthly between July and September. The $48.6 million traded during this period primarily consisted of stablecoins. Stablecoins are frequently used in developing countries experiencing significant currency devaluation or foreign currency shortages.

Stablecoin adoption gained further traction in Bolivia in October 2024 when Banco Bisa, a local bank, introduced a stablecoin custody service. Supported by the country's financial regulator, this service enables residents to buy, sell, and trade Tether's USDt (USDT), a stablecoin pegged to the US dollar. Cointelegraph previously reported in September 2016 that Bolivia stood to benefit significantly from cryptocurrency adoption, as a large portion of the population was unbanked, with only 11% using debit cards and 5% using credit cards. Despite these potential benefits, Bolivia maintained its ban on cryptocurrencies until 2024, labeling them as pyramid schemes in May 2017 and arresting crypto advocates.

data.title: Bitcoin surpasses Saudi Aramco to become the eighth largest asset in the world
data.description: Odaily Planet Daily News: According to CompaniesMarketcap data, as the price of Bitcoin rebounded to the $85,000 range, its market value has now risen to $1.677 trillion, with a daily increase of 4.51%, surpassing Saudi Aramco (US$1.675 trillion) to rank eighth in the global asset market value.

data.title: Crypto.com Enters UAE Derivatives Market After Securing VARA Approval
data.description:
Crypto.com has secured a limited license from Dubai’s Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority (VARA) to offer derivatives trading in the UAE.

The post Crypto.com Enters UAE Derivatives Market After Securing VARA Approval first appeared on Ecoinimist.

data.title: Analyst: Whales bought more than 20,000 BTC in the past 48 hours
data.description:
Odaily Planet Daily News According to data disclosed by crypto analyst Ali Martinez, whales bought more than 20,000 BTC in the past 48 hours.
The market shows that BTC broke through 85,000 USDT in a short period of time today and is now reported at 84,942.1 USDT, with a 24-hour increase of 4.7%.

data.title: Bitcoin Surges as Whales Accumulate Over 20,000 BTC
data.description: According to Odaily, data revealed by crypto analyst Ali Martinez indicates that whales have purchased more than 20,000 BTC in the past 48 hours. The market shows that Bitcoin briefly surpassed 85,000 USDT today and is currently priced at 84,942.1 USDT, marking a 24-hour increase of 4.7%.

data.title: Hong Kong's Blockchain Sector Sees 250% Growth Since 2022
data.description:
According to PANews, a recent report by Hong Kong's InvestHK and its partners reveals a significant 250% growth in Hong Kong's blockchain applications and software industry since 2022. This surge underscores Hong Kong's emerging role as a leading center for fintech and digital asset development in Asia.

The report analyzed 1,100 fintech companies operating in Hong Kong as of July 2024, highlighting substantial growth across various fintech sub-sectors. Currently, Hong Kong hosts 175 blockchain companies, with the applications/software sector experiencing remarkable expansion over the past two years. Additionally, the digital assets and cryptocurrency segment has increased by nearly 30%.

Hong Kong now boasts the highest number of fintech companies in areas such as wealth technology, blockchain, digital assets and cryptocurrencies, payments and remittances, and other fintech enterprise solutions.
